title: Restrict the King
setup: A passed pawn on **d5** marches toward promotion, with the black king watching from e8.
hint: Push the pawn to a square the king cannot enter. Restriction wins endgames.
explanation:
## The Idea

This is a **king restriction** technique — a critical idea in pawn endings. By pushing the pawn forward, White denies the black king key squares.

White plays {{d6}}.

## What the Pawn Controls

A pawn on d6 attacks the squares **c7** and **e7**. The black king on e8 now cannot legally step to e7 — that square is attacked.

Black's king is forced to retreat or shuffle: {{Kd7}} (blockading the pawn), {{Kf7}}, {{Kf8}}, or {{Kd8}}.

## Why d6 (Not d-Push Slower)?

In this position the white king is far back on e1, so the pawn cannot promote on its own — it needs the king's help. **But** advancing the pawn first restricts the black king and creates time for the white king to march up the board.

## Pattern to Memorize

> In **K+P vs K** endings, the pawn doesn't always go fastest by pushing. Sometimes it goes fastest by **restricting** — denying the enemy king key squares so your king can outflank.

This is the idea behind **opposition**, **outflanking**, and the famous **square of the pawn** — building blocks of every pawn endgame.
